goal: PBT for context retrieval Properties 2, 3, 5: classifier determinism, supersession ranking, anti-pattern conservation

summary.title: PBT for the three under-tested context-retrieval invariants
summary.what: Add three rapid-driven property tests in `internal/engine/context_retrieval_property_test.go` covering Properties 2, 3, and 5 from spec §9 Step 2 — the three invariants that the original Step 2 PR tested only by example. Build-tagged `!quick` to match the existing PBT pattern, so quick-test skips them and the full suite picks them up. PBTs: TestPropertyClassifyRetrievalStatusDeterministic (Property 2 — pure-function determinism), TestPropertyAntiPatternsConservation (Property 5 — anti-patterns survive retrieval verbatim regardless of view shape), TestPropertySupersessionRanking (Property 3 — superseder always ranks above superseded).
summary.why: agent.md (the project's design protocol) Step 3 says 'List the Invariants. Describe Property-based Testing test-case logic.' The Step 2 PR declared six properties but shipped only example-based tests. Three of those properties were genuinely under-tested — particularly Property 5 (anti-patterns conservation) which is the load-bearing safety claim. A user asked for a PBT review; the honest answer was 'PBT is incomplete' and the right fix is to ship the missing PBTs.
summary.user_goal: Honour the design protocol: invariants the spec calls 'load-bearing' get property-based coverage, not just one example each.

decisions.rationale: Properties 1 (anti-pattern empty-why rejected) and 6 (guidance per status) are exhaustive switches in the implementation — every branch is already covered by the table-driven example tests. Property 4 (abandoned visible) is a single boolean and the existing example covers it. Properties 2, 3, 5 have non-trivial input spaces and were tested by single examples — exactly where PBT pays off.

decisions.chose: `!quick` so make quick-test skips them; full `go test` picks them up.
decisions.rationale: Existing PBT pattern in property_test.go uses the same tag, and the tests run ~42s — slow enough to hurt the inner-loop test cycle, fast enough that CI / pre-merge runs them every time.

decisions.point: synthetic view vs full seal flow
decisions.chose: Build MainlineView in memory and write it directly via Store.WriteMainlineView for retrieval-level PBTs.
decisions.rationale: The full seal flow (start/append/seal/sync) is expensive and the PBTs would run in minutes, not seconds. The retrieval logic only reads the materialised view, so going through the seal flow tests something other than the property under test.

decisions.chose: Pure-function determinism + canonical-status check (call 6×, assert all agree, assert the answer is one of four canonical strings).
decisions.rationale: Determinism is the easiest hidden-state regression to surface (a global RNG, a clock read inside the classifier would flicker across calls). The canonical-status check guards the JSON contract — a classifier branch that returned a non-listed string would land in agent JSON output and confuse readers.

rejected.reason: Already exhaustive via existing tests. Adding rapid-driven cases on top would just slow the suite without finding regressions — those properties' input spaces are small and discrete.

rejected.alternative: Run the PBTs through the full seal flow.
rejected.reason: Tests something other than the property under test. PBTs need to surface the property's failure modes cheaply; going through seal/sync introduces noise from unrelated subsystems.

rejected.alternative: Skip PBT, claim the example tests are enough.
rejected.reason: The user asked for PBT review specifically; the spec promised PBT in Step 3 of agent.md. 'Already enough' is what got us into this audit.

risks: PBT seeds are deterministic per rapid release; an upgrade to rapid that changes seed behaviour could surface a previously-unfound case as a regression. Acceptable: that's exactly the noise PBT is supposed to find.

Synthetic-view PBT path doesn't exercise the full seal/sync pipeline, so a regression in that pipeline that bypasses the view-write would not be caught here. Mitigation: existing example tests in context_retrieval_test.go go through the real flow.

anti_patterns.what: Adding PBT-style names to example tests instead of writing real generative tests
anti_patterns.why: PBT's value is in adversarial input search, not in the function name. A test called TestPropertyXxx that runs one fixed case provides false confidence — readers assume rapid is exploring the space when it isn't.
Slow inner-loop kills the dev cycle. Existing pattern uses build tag `!quick` for a reason; the full suite is where PBTs belong.

followups: Lint determinism PBT: run LintIntent twice on the same input, assert identical issue list. Cheap to add; would catch any future hidden-state in the lint rules.
AntiPattern validation PBT: random what/why/severity inputs, assert ValidateSealResult agrees with the rule list (empty why → reject, etc).

Classifier branch coverage: rapid does not measure branch coverage; consider adding a small property that asserts each of the four canonical statuses can be reached for some input, so dead-code regressions surface.

architectural_claims: Properties declared in the design doc but tested only by example are under-tested; rapid PBT is the canonical fix.
Build-tag `!quick` is the project convention for slow tests; PBTs live there to keep the inner-loop fast.
behavioral_changes: Three new tests in the full test suite. quick-test unchanged (tag-gated).
Total run time of full engine suite increases by ~42s for the three PBTs.
